topic: A student learns their thesis advisor is leaving the university
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. Professor Albright was leaving. The official announcement was terse, a formality for a transition that felt seismic to my world. I stared at the screen, the words blurring into meaningless shapes. My hands, resting on the cool wooden desk, began to tremble slightly. I reread the message, searching for a hidden caveat, a sign that this was some kind of elaborate prank. But there was only the finality of it. A sudden, sharp pressure built behind my ribs, making it hard to draw a full breath. I pushed my chair back, the scrape of the metal legs against the floor sounding deafening in the quiet library. I needed air. I walked to the window, looking out at the manicured quad, feeling an unfamiliar hollowness bloom in my chest.

[story 2]

"So, he's actually going?" I managed to ask, the sound of my own voice thin and reedy. The words felt clumsy on my tongue, inadequate for the magnitude of the news. Dr. Vance, my guide through the labyrinth of my dissertation, was departing. I kept fiddling with the edge of my notebook, the paper rough beneath my fingertips. My gaze kept flicking back to the office door, a solid barrier suddenly seeming impossibly distant. A coldness crept up my neck, a sensation that made the fine hairs on my arms prickle. I started pacing the length of my cramped dorm room, my footsteps echoing unevenly. Every shadow seemed to deepen, and the silence of the room amplified the frantic thumping in my ears.

[story 3]

I spent the entire afternoon meticulously re-reading old correspondence, searching for some misplaced reassurance that this was temporary. Every carefully worded sentence from Professor Albright felt suddenly weighted with impending absence. When the final confirmation arrived, I didn't move for a long time. I sat motionless, the weight of the news settling heavily onto my shoulders, making my posture rigid. My jaw tightened until my teeth ached. I finally stood up, moving with a slow, deliberate stiffness. I walked to the window overlooking the darkened campus grounds, watching the distant lights flicker on. A deep, involuntary sigh escaped me, a sound heavy with unspoken anxiety, and I pressed the heels of my hands against my eyes, trying to physically block out the sight of the world continuing without him.
